title: Record refinancing surge looms as latest rate rise hits homeowners
full_text: Other Editions 07 May 2026 property Record refinancing surge looms as latest rate rise hits homeowners Canberra Times Nina Hendy 7 May 2026 657 words English CANBTZ © 2026 The Canberra Times Refinancing is set to surge again this year as the nation's mortgage holders brace for impact as another interest rate rise hits hard. Record numbers of Australians refinanced last year, with the Australian Bureau of Statistics data revealing that more than 640,000 homeowners refinanced their mortgage in 2025. Mortgage brokers report a 60 per cent rise in refinancing inquiries over the past six months, while 80 per cent of borrowers say they are highly concerned about cost of living pressures and future rate rises. The data, from Compare Club, reveals that mortgage stress is increasingly being driven by repayments, not property prices, with brokers surveyed noting serviceability is becoming a bigger issue. Inflationary pressures Inflation is proving more challenging for the Reserve Bank of Australia to get under control than expected, forcing the board to yank on the most effective and yet brutal monetary policy lever that regulators have within their control - rate rises. Unfortunately, it will be a body blow for mortgaged homeowners. Despite calls from experts for the Reserve Bank not to raise interest rates, all four banks had tipped that the cash rate would be pushed up to 4.35 per cent. It won't be easy going. Compare Club analysis shows that new modelling suggests even a 0.25 per cent rate rise could add $91 a month to a $600,000 mortgage, $122 a month to an $800,000 mortgage and $152 a month to a $1 million mortgage. Richard Whitten, home loans expert at Finder, said the decision to hit homeowners where it hurts is a devastating blow for many homeowners already doing it tough. Finder's data shows that 39 per cent of homeowners are struggling to pay their mortgage in April, up from 35 per cent in January this year. "Being back at a 4.35 per cent cash rate will feel like a big backward step for those with a mortgage. While this was the peak two years ago, the cost of living has continued to climb since then, meaning the same rate now carries a much heavier weight," Mr Whitten said. He encouraged homeowners carrying a mortgage to explore their options. "If you haven't refinanced or asked your lender for a better deal, you're almost certainly paying too much." Poor decision, some say Not everyone believes that lifting the cash rate was the right decision. Roy Morgan says the Reserve Bank has ignored key economic indicators that show the economy is already in a weakened state, and that the nation could perhaps already be in recession. In a statement issued this week, it says a rate rise will most likely plunge the nation into a recession we don't have to have. However, Michael Yardney of Metropole Property Strategists insists that there's only so much the RBA can do when inflation is being driven by global factors like energy prices or supply chain disruptions. "Higher interest rates mainly dampen local demand, they don't fix the root cause, so their real role is to keep inflation expectations under control and stop temporary shocks from becoming embedded in wages and prices," Ms Yardney said. Before refinancing, homeowners need to consider more than just the cash rate. The switch may provide you with a reduction to overall mortgage repayments, access to better features or a chance to consolidate debt but exit fees could make a move to another lender less appealing. Meanwhile, Ray White Real Estate senior economist Nerida Conisbee is contemplating the impact of a cash rate lift on property markets, which she says will be mixed. "Higher rates will reduce borrowing capacity and weigh on buyer demand. But Australia's housing shortage remains severe. Strong population growth and weak construction activity means prices and rents are likely to remain supported, even as higher rates slow momentum," Ms Conisbee said. summary: A significant surge in mortgage refinancing is anticipated as Australian homeowners react to the latest interest rate rise, with over 640,000 refinancing in 2025. Mortgage stress is increasingly attributed to repayment pressures rather than property prices, with many homeowners struggling to meet their mortgage obligations. Experts suggest that homeowners should explore refinancing options to mitigate rising costs, despite concerns about the broader economic implications of increased rates.

heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Refinancing inquiries have risen by 60% in the past six months due to increased interest rates.
39% of homeowners reported difficulty in paying their mortgage, reflecting growing financial strain.
Experts advise homeowners to consider refinancing options to reduce repayment burdens.
relevance: Understanding the implications of rising interest rates and refinancing trends is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals in assessing market risks and advising clients.
